figures.gloss: The central messianic figure of Tanna, Vanuatu. Emerging in the late 1930s, he urged the rejection of Christian missionary teachings and colonial labor, promising a utopian era where colonizers would depart and ancestors would return with endless 'kago' (cargo).
figures.facet: He is an anti-colonial savior whose utopia relies on reclaiming indigenous custom (kastom) while paradoxically inheriting the industrialized wealth of the oppressor, synthesizing traditional spirit-mediumship with WWII military aesthetics.
figures.provenance: Anthropological accounts of Tanna, notably Peter Worsley's 'The Trumpet Shall Sound' (1957) and Joel Bonnemaison's fieldwork.

figures.gloss: Industrial goods—canned meat, radios, jeeps, and rifles—understood not as manufactured commodities, but as the metaphysical fruit of ancestral labor. It is the ultimate manifestation of spiritual favor, rightful inheritance, and ancestral love.
figures.facet: Westerners view these goods as secular products of capitalism; cargo movements recognize them as inherently spiritual objects. The tragedy of colonialism is that white men deceitfully intercepted the cargo intended for the Melanesians.
figures.provenance: Peter Lawrence's 'Road Belong Cargo' (1964), focusing on the Madang District of Papua New Guinea.

figures.gloss: The highly active stratovolcano on Tanna. In the cosmology of the John Frum movement, its fiery crater is not just a geographic feature but the subterranean spirit-city and cosmic forge where John Frum and his army manufacture the prophesied cargo.
figures.facet: It recontextualizes the traditional underworld of the dead into a massive, industrialized ancestral factory. The spirits do not rest; they run assembly lines beneath the magma, preparing the eschatological fleet.
figures.provenance: Fieldwork by Jean Guiart (1956) and Joel Bonnemaison (1994) on Tanna's spiritual geography.

figures.gloss: The rigorous clearing of jungle to build replica runways, complete with bamboo control towers, wooden headphones, and flare-lit perimeters. Practitioners mimic the precise bodily movements of WWII signalmen to guide in the ancestral cargo planes.
figures.facet: It is the ultimate expression of sympathetic magic applied to modern logistics. By perfectly replicating the ritual infrastructure of American GIs, practitioners believe they can compel the heavens to yield material miracles.
figures.provenance: Widespread post-WWII Melanesian accounts; famously documented in David Attenborough's 'The Quest in Paradise' (1960).

figures.gloss: Venerated by the Yaohnanen people of Tanna as the pale-skinned son of the mountain spirit. According to prophecy, he traveled over the sea to marry a powerful queen and would eventually return to his homeland, bringing peace and immense prosperity.
figures.facet: He is a living European royal entirely subsumed into an indigenous mythos. The movement reframes the British Crown not as an imperial authority, but as a subordinate appendage to Tanna's spiritual sovereignty. His death in April 2021 did not close the prophecy: Yaohnanen villagers held mourning rites and, per contemporaneous press coverage, debated whether his spirit had begun its journey home or whether the promised return remained still to come.
figures.provenance: Anthropological tracking of the Yaohnanen movement since the 1970s; Matthew Baylis, 'Man Belong Mrs Queen' (2013); Reuters and BBC coverage of Yaohnanen mourning rites, April 2021.

figures.gloss: The mythical Papuan creator-figure of the Koreri movement in Biak. An old man who rejuvenated himself in fire, he departed after humanity failed to heed him, promising to return and initiate 'Koreri'—a utopian era without death, sickness, or colonial subjugation.
figures.facet: He bridges deep indigenous mythology with explosive political resistance. His promise of a returning golden age fueled actual violent uprisings against Japanese and Dutch colonizers, transmuting a myth of skin-shedding into anti-colonial warfare.
figures.provenance: F.C. Kamma's 'Koreri: Messianic Movements in the Biak-Numfor Culture Area' (1972).

figures.gloss: A mass millenarian movement in the Gulf Province of Papua characterized by 'head-heaving', speaking in tongues, and the destruction of traditional cult objects. Adherents prepared for a great ghost ship carrying deceased relatives and white men's goods.
figures.facet: It represents a radical, iconoclastic break with the past. Rather than preserving tradition, practitioners destroyed their own sacred masks, believing total cultural erasure was the necessary price of admission to the industrialized ancestral utopia.
figures.provenance: Francis Edgar Williams's 'The Vailala Madness and the Destruction of Native Ceremonies in the Gulf Division' (1923).

figures.gloss: A syncretic spirit figure on Tanna, largely inspired by the presence of segregated African American troops during WWII. Viewed as an egalitarian, generous brother-figure who operates in tandem with John Frum to bypass white colonial authorities and deliver wealth.
figures.facet: He reflects the profound ontological shock of seeing black men wielding the same industrial wealth and military power as white colonizers, proving that 'kago' was not biologically restricted to white skin.
figures.provenance: Anthropological accounts of Tanna's post-war mythos, particularly Lamont Lindstrom's 'Cargo Cult: Strange Stories of Desire from Melanesia and Beyond' (1993).

figures.gloss: A decorated WWII sergeant-major of the Madang District (Papua New Guinea) whose post-war cargo rehabilitation scheme, administered on the colonial government's behalf, was reinterpreted by followers as a messianic cargo movement.
figures.facet: A reluctant messiah: the man himself worked within the colonial administration to deliver development programs, while his followers read every one of his acts as coded proof he possessed the secret of cargo.
figures.provenance: Peter Lawrence, 'Road Belong Cargo' (1964) — already the roster's cited source for the kago concept.

figures.gloss: A Manus Island leader whose 'New Way' movement (from 1946) fused social and political reform with millenarian expectation of ancestral cargo; became the largest and most institutionally durable Melanesian movement of its kind.
figures.facet: The case where cargo thinking shaded into functioning micro-state building — the New Way produced villages, courts, and a political party, not only prophecy, even as an ecstatic offshoot ('The Noise') kept the ancestor-cargo expectation alive within it.
figures.provenance: Margaret Mead, 'New Lives for Old' (1956); Peter Worsley, 'The Trumpet Shall Sound' (1957).

figures.gloss: A Fijian oracle-priest of the 1880s who prophesied the return of the ancestors and the reversal of the colonial order; his Tuka movement is the earliest well-documented Melanesian millenarian movement, predating the tradition's own stated 1885 start date.
figures.facet: The overlooked point of origin: everything the roster's later cargo movements do — ancestor-return prophecy, ritual rejection of colonial order — Navosavakadua's Tuka already did decades before John Frum, before the aircraft and jeeps later cults fixate on even existed.
figures.provenance: Deported by the British colonial administration; documented in Fiji colonial-era records and subsequent Pacific historiography of the Tuka movement.

figures.gloss: The annual John Frum Day at Sulphur Bay, Tanna: drill parades with bamboo rifles, 'USA' painted on bare chests, and dances that reenact the expected return of John Frum and his cargo.
figures.facet: A liturgical calendar built entirely from the memory of a few years of American wartime presence — the feast keeps 1942 permanently reenactable, decades after the airfields it commemorates were abandoned.
figures.provenance: Widely documented at Sulphur Bay, Tanna; Kirk Huffman's and Lamont Lindstrom's Vanuatu fieldwork.

figures.gloss: Red wooden crosses, adapted from WWII military-medical insignia, planted to fence and mark John Frum's sacred sites on Tanna.
figures.facet: A borrowed bureaucratic symbol of Western medicine, stripped of its original organizational meaning and recoded as the mark of the coming dispensation of healing and goods — the emblem outlived the institution that issued it.
figures.provenance: Fieldwork documentation of Sulphur Bay shrines; Lamont Lindstrom, 'Cargo Cult: Strange Stories of Desire from Melanesia and Beyond' (1993); Joel Bonnemaison's Tanna fieldwork.

edges.note: Intra-tradition: The twin saviors of Tanna; one the architect of custom, the other the black American intercessor of wealth.
Intra-tradition: The messiah and his fiery, subterranean industrial forge.
Intra-tradition: The spiritual commodity and the physical, sympathetic infrastructure built to coax it from the sky.
Intra-tradition: Where John Frum demands a strict return to indigenous custom, the Vailala Madness demanded the total destruction of traditional artifacts.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are eschatological saviors expected to return at the end of time to banish oppressors and restore a golden age.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve the mythic narrative of a culturally foundational figure expected to return from over the sea.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: John Frum's prophecies heavily borrow from, and subvert, Christian missionary teachings of the Second Coming and apocalypse.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an ultimate, magical source of endless material prosperity and abundance that is fought over and coveted.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both center on a blazing volcanic underworld re-imagined as an active, bustling forge where divine items are manufactured.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mythic smith whose metallurgical mastery equips the divine hosts, dwelling in an otherworldly domain.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve constructing localized architecture that perfectly maps to, and attempts to draw down, the cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The divine son or king who departs the earthly realm to rule afar, whose eventual return promises cosmic renewal.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Ancient figures associated with a primordial golden age who retreat, promising their utopian realm will eventually encompass the earth again.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A radiant figure whose return from a state of departure signals the rebirth of the world after a catastrophic age.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both induce a localized, ecstatic madness that shatters conventional social order and demands total surrender to the spirit.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Powerful, iron-wielding, martial figures associated with modern industry, labor, and the fierce protection of the oppressed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Operating as a brotherly dyad (with John Frum) that outwits the lords of the current world order to secure survival.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Mass ecstatic bodily movements and trembling utilized as the primary technology for contacting the spirit realm.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A wealthy, distant chief of the horizon who hoards massive resources and must be spiritually negotiated with.
Divinized figures of the colonized who are awaited back — Rizal the returning king and John Frum the returning provider, each holding the promise that the colonial order will be overturned.
Yali is the actual historical figure behind the roster's own kago citation (Lawrence, 'Road Belong Cargo') — the source made an entity.
Contemporaneous 1940s Melanesian movement-founders responding to the same wartime cargo shock, one building durable political institutions (Manus), the other a standing ritual liturgy (Tanna).
The earliest documented Melanesian ancestor-return prophet prefigures the John Frum pattern by roughly six decades.
contemporaneous 1880s indigenous oracle-prophets, both deported or suppressed by colonial authorities, both promising the return of the dead and reversal of colonial dispossession.
The annual liturgical feast keeping the messiah's promised return perpetually reenactable.
The calendar feast and the sympathetic-magic infrastructure it annually reactivates.
The visual emblem marking the messiah's sacred sites on Tanna.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: two contemporary deification cults built around a real, named, publicly documented human — one Argentine football, one Melanesian colonial encounter — each with its own founding narrative and ongoing devotional practice.

dossier.essence: A family of Melanesian millenarian movements — in Papua New Guinea, Vanuatu, the Solomons, and western New Guinea — arising under colonial rule from the late nineteenth century and intensifying around the World Wars, in which prophets announced that ancestors or a savior figure would return bearing 'cargo': the manufactured wealth Europeans possessed but were never seen to make. The John Frum movement on Tanna and the Yaohnanen veneration of Prince Philip survive as living communities. The label 'cargo cult' itself is a colonial-era coinage (first in print in 1945) that many anthropologists now treat as a distorting category rather than a neutral description.
dossier.cosmos: The movements share a moral economy of the cosmos: manufactured goods are produced or sanctioned by the ancestors, so their maldistribution is a spiritual scandal — the whites intercepted what the dead intended for their own kin. An imminent reversal (Koreri on Biak, John Frum's promised day on Tanna) will return the dead, abolish colonial labor and money, and open an age of abundance; in Biak's Manseren myth the millennium includes bodily renewal, imaged as the shedding of skin. The eschatology is emphatically this-worldly and near.
dossier.practice: Prophet-led and episodic: communities have destroyed gardens, pigs, and money to force the new age; cleared airstrips and built bamboo control towers, warehouses, and flagpoles in ritual replication of colonial and military logistics; drilled as uniformed 'armies' (John Frum Day is kept each 15 February on Tanna); and in the 1919 Vailala movement, amid mass trembling, abandoned and destroyed their own traditional cult objects. What the movements ask is radical divestment now against total abundance to come.
dossier.canon: No texts of their own — authority is prophetic and oral; the documentary corpus is ethnographic, above all F. E. Williams on Vailala (1923), Worsley's 'The Trumpet Shall Sound' (1957), Lawrence's 'Road Belong Cargo' (1964), and Kamma's 'Koreri' (1972).
dossier.reflects: Religion as a theory of production and just distribution — the sacred invoked to explain where wealth comes from and to whom it is rightfully owed.
sources: Peter Worsley, 'The Trumpet Shall Sound: A Study of Cargo Cults in Melanesia' (1957)
Lamont Lindstrom, 'Cargo Cult: Strange Stories of Desire from Melanesia and Beyond' (1993)
F. C. Kamma, 'Koreri: Messianic Movements in the Biak-Numfor Culture Area' (1972)
